items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.correct: The small projecting line at the end of letter strokes.
items.misconception: A whole typeface that looks formal.
items.why: Students conflate the FEATURE (the small foot/stroke) with the CLASSIFICATION (serif typefaces, which have them). Sans serif means 'without serifs' - those little projections at letter endings.

items.q: What is the central innovation of Cubism?
items.correct: Showing an object from multiple viewpoints simultaneously on a flat surface.
items.why: Students take the name literally. Cubism is named for facet-like fragmentation, but it isn't about cubes - it's about breaking the single Renaissance viewpoint into many at once.

items.q: Whose triadic sign distinguishes icon, index and symbol?
items.why: It's Peirce. “Saussure” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Whose 1964 Rhetoric of the Image analysed Panzani's pasta ad?
items.why: Correct answer: Barthes. “Metz” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Mitchell publish Picture Theory?
items.why: Exact answer: 1994 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
